coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of trinitrotoluene in water samples based on a natural mineral attapulgite modified electrode
coredata.prism:issueName: Special issue in honor of Chinese Academician Prof. Hong-Yuan Chen for his 80th birthday
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Ping
Deng, Dan
Zhang, Hui
Cai, Chenxin
coredata.dc:description: This study reports an electrochemical approach for an assay of trinitrotoluene (TNT) in water samples. This assay is based on the electrochemical reduction of TNT at a natural mineral, attapulgite, modified glassy carbon (attapulgite/GC) electrode. To detect TNT, TNT is first accumulated on an attapulgite/GC electrode by holding the electrode at a constant potential of 0V versus Ag/AgCl for 40s in an aqueous TNT solution. Next, the attapulgite/GC electrode with accumulated TNT is transferred to a fresh PBS solution (0.1M, pH7.0, without TNT), where the TNT reduction current at −0.36V versus Ag/AgCl in a linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) shows a linear response to TNT concentrations in the aqueous solution from 1 to 200ppb with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 and a detection limit of 0.2ppb at a signal/noise (S/N) of 3. Moreover, the attapulgite/GC electrode displays high stability, good anti-interference ability to certain common interferents in real water samples, and reliable detection precision. Along with the abundance, low price, and environmental friendliness of attapulgite, the developed electrochemical sensor for the TNT assay has great potential for the practical determination of TNT levels in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Linear sweep voltammetry
Chemically modified electrode
Attapulgite
Trinitrotoluene
Water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the attapulgite modified electrode
Electrochemical detection of TNT using the attapulgite/GC electrode
Detecting the TNT in water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical XRD patterns (A), and TEM image of the purified attapulgite (B). (C) The SEM image of the attapulgite modified glassy carbon electrode surface.

(A) FTIR spectra of pure attapulgite (curve a), TNT (curve b), and TNT-adsorbed attapulgite (curve c). (B) UV-vis spectra of pure attapulgite (curve a), 100ppb TNT after (curve b) and before being accumulated by attapulgite (curve c).

LSV of the attapulgite/GC (a) and GC (b) electrode in PBS (0.1M, pH7.0, without TNT). Before recording the LSV, the electrodes were accumulated in 100ppb TNT solution for 40s at 0V. (c) depicts the LSV of the attapulgite/GC electrode without loading the TNT in PBS (without TNT). Scan rate: 50mV/s.

The dependence of reduction current density at −0.36V (vs. Ag/AgCl) on (A) accumulation time and (B) buffer pH. Before recording the LSV, the electrodes were accumulated in 100ppb TNT solution for 40s at 0V. (C) LSV of the attapulgite/GC electrode in PBS after the electrode was accumulated for 40s at a constant potential of 0V in the different concentrations of TNT. TNT concentration is (from curve a to m): 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000ppb. Scan rate is 50mV/s. (D) The dependence of TNT reduction current density at −0.36V (vs. Ag/AgCl) on the concentration of TNT.

LSV of the attapulgite/GC electrode in PBS (0.1M, pH7.0) after the electrode was accumulated in 100ppb 2,4-DNT (A, curve b) and 2-NT (B, curve b) solution for 40s at potential of 0V (vs. Ag/AgCl). The curve (a) depicts the LSV of the attapulgite/GC electrode in PBS (0.1M, pH7.0) without accumulated in 100ppb 2,4-DNT (A) and 2-NT (B). Scan rate: 50mV/s.
Comparison of the detection limit for analysis of TNT by different sensor system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of TNT in three water samples using the attapulgite/GC electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values determined by the attapulgite/GC electrode; it is an average value of the response of five measurements for each sample.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work is dedicated to Professor Hong-Yuan Chen on the occasion of his 80th birthday and in honor of his contributions to electroanalytical chemistry.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of trinitrotoluene in water samples based on a natural mineral attapulgite modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Power Batteries, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210097, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Power Batteries
Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports an electrochemical approach for an assay of trinitrotoluene (TNT) in water samples. This assay is based on the electrochemical reduction of TNT at a natural mineral, attapulgite, modified glassy carbon (attapulgite/GC) electrode. To detect TNT, TNT is first accumulated on an attapulgite/GC electrode by holding the electrode at a constant potential of 0V versus Ag/AgCl for 40s in an aqueous TNT solution. Next, the attapulgite/GC electrode with accumulated TNT is transferred to a fresh PBS solution (0.1M, pH7.0, without TNT), where the TNT reduction current at −0.36V versus Ag/AgCl in a linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) shows a linear response to TNT concentrations in the aqueous solution from 1 to 200ppb with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 and a detection limit of 0.2ppb at a signal/noise (S/N) of 3. Moreover, the attapulgite/GC electrode displays high stability, good anti-interference ability to certain common interferents in real water samples, and reliable detection precision. Along with the abundance, low price, and environmental friendliness of attapulgite, the developed electrochemical sensor for the TNT assay has great potential for the practical determination of TNT levels in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrotoluene (TNT) is one of the most environmentally hazardous chemicals; even trace quantities of TNT can cause detrimental effects to the environment and human health [1–8]. Soil and water at sites where TNT has been manufactured, stored, or extensively used and tested are physically disturbed and persistently corrosive and contaminated. Furthermore, the environment contaminated with TNT is difficult to be remediated because of the chemically inert nature of TNT [9,10]. TNT is also a biological hazard, as environmental TNT is a mutagen and carcinogen that can be absorbed by the skin and gastrointestinal tract. Therefore, TNT is toxic to human health, causing anemia as well as abnormal functioning of the human liver, spleen, circulatory and immune systems, and can even cause cancer [5,11,12]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency recommends a maximum allowable level of TNT in drinking water to be 2ppb [13]. Therefore, analytical methods used for sensing TNT are required to achieve a detection limit lower than 2ppb.

Many conventional analytical methods have been used for detecting and assaying TNT, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), which is the approach recommended by the Environmental Protection Agency for assaying TNT [13], gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [14], and ion mobility spectroscopy (IMS) [15], etc. Along with these conventional methods, recently, several new techniques have been developed for detecting and assaying TNT, including colorimetry [16], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [17,18], fluorescence spectroscopy [19,20], and luminescent spectroscopy [21], among others. All of these methods enable a sensitive assay of TNT but require bulky expensive instruments, long assay time, and well-trained laboratory technicians. Thus, there is a need for a compact, low-cost, and simple approach for detecting and assaying TNT in the field.

An electrochemical approach can meet this need. Electrochemical techniques have many advantages, such as high selectivity, low detection limit, rapid analysis, portability, ease of use, and low cost [22–29]; in addition, these advantages can be employed for performing a TNT assay with high fidelity.

Electrochemical detection of TNT is based on the electrochemical reduction of the nitro group (NO2) in the TNT molecule to an amine group (NH2) [30]. The rate and extent of the electrochemical reduction of the nitro group significantly depends on the nature of the electrode material. The ideal electrode material should adsorb TNT and catalyze the electrochemical reduction of TNT. Many types of materials have been investigated for the electrochemical reduction of TNT, including various carbon materials (such as glass carbon [22], carbon nanotube [22], graphene sheet [19,23,25], nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots [24], and boron-doped diamond [31]), gold [32], silica [33], and metal-oxides [34,35], etc. It has been determined that electrode materials with high adsorption ability for TNT can improve TNT detection sensitivity. For example, the electrochemical activity of TNT reduction on the ionic liquid-modified graphene (IL-graphene) was 51.4-folds higher than that on glassy carbon (GC) [22] because IL-graphene has a strong tendency to accumulate nitroaromatic compounds on its surface via π–π interaction between the two-dimensional planar structure of graphene and the nitroaromatic structure [36]. Moreover, the high electronic conductivity of IL-graphene can facilitate electron transfer during the electrochemical reaction process. Our recent work demonstrated that the doping of nitrogen in graphene can significantly enhance its electrochemical activity for TNT reduction, as indicated by the fact that the amplitude of the cathodic peaks of TNT reduction on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is ~6.5 times higher than that obtained on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [24]. The higher electrochemical activity of the N-GQDs for TNT reduction is ascribed to the enhanced TNT adsorption ability and improved electronic characteristics of N-GQDs. Our theoretical calculation indicated that nitrogen doping can reduce the energy gap of HOMO–LUMO (H–L gap) of the N-GQDs as well as enhance their electrochemical activity and electrical conductivity [24]. Moreover, nitrogen doping causes the delocalization of the highest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) spatial, leading to an improved adsorption of TNT. However, the synthesis of the N-GQDs is not straightforward and usually requires tedious procedures. Moreover, they cannot be synthesized in scalable and reproducible quantities.

Herein, we report the use of the nanometer-sized natural mineral, attapulgite, as a novel substrate to accumulate TNT and as a unique electrode material to electrochemically detect TNT. Attapulgite is a hydrated magnesium silicate consisting of double chains of SiO, which are further linked by a layer of octahedral magnesium atoms in 6-fold coordination, forming a network of strips that are joined together only along the edges [37–46]. The natural mineral has advantages of non-toxicity, good biological compatibility, low cost, abundance in nature, and in particular, high surface area (169m2/g [38]), and has been extensively used in the fields of pharmaceutical industries [39,40], nanocomposite preparations [42], environmental absorbents [46], catalyst supports [37,43–45], etc. Our results indicate that TNT can be strongly adsorbed on the surface of attapulgite and exhibits high electrochemical reduction characteristics. The dependence of the electrochemical reduction current on TNT concentration can be used to sensitively sense TNT in water samples as low as 0.2ppb, which is considerably lower than the maximum allowable level of TNT (2ppb) in drinking water recommended by the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the attapulgite modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to use, attapulgite (Jiuchuan Clay Company, Xuyi, China) was purified according to the previously reported procedures [41]. In brief, it was ground in a KM-10 planetary mill (Nanjing University Instrument Plant, China) at 800rpm for 2h. The ground sample (1g) was then treated with 100mL of HCl (8M) at 80°C for 2h and cooled to ambient temperature. The suspension was filtered through a 0.65-μm microporous nylon membrane and thoroughly washed with double distilled water. The product was dried at 80°C for 12h in air to obtain the purified sample (white powder). X-ray (XRD) diffraction patterns of the purified attapulgite show peaks at ~8.5, 13.9, 19.8, and 28.1 ° (Fig. 1A), which are characteristic patterns of pure attapulgite and correspond to (110), (020), (400), and (040) plane, respectively. All of these diffraction peaks are the same as those of the reference patterns collected for attapulgite (JCPDS 29–0855). The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image indicates that attapulgite has a rod-like morphology with an average size of 20–50nm in width and 0.5–1μm in length (Fig. 1B). The zeta potential (ζ) of the purified attapulgite was measured to be approx. –28±2mV. The polydispersity index (PDI) of the attapulgite was estimated to be ~0.35 based on dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements.

Prior to fabricating the modified electrode, the glassy carbon electrode (GC, 3mm in diameter, CH Instruments) was sequentially polished with metallographic abrasive paper (No. 6) and slurries of 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina to create a mirror finish. The GC electrode was then sonicated for approximately 1min in absolute ethanol and then double-distilled water to remove traces of alumina grit from the electrode surface. After being rinsed thoroughly with double distilled water, the GC was dried in air at ambient temperature. For the fabrication of the attapulgite modified GC electrode, attapulgite was first dispersed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH7.0) by ultrasonication (at a concentration of 1mg/mL). The suspension (8μL) was later mixed with Nafion (2μL), and finally the mixture (5μL) was cast onto the surface of the GC electrode with a microsyringe. The solvent was allowed to be evaporated in air at ambient temperature. This electrode is denoted as the attapulgite/GC electrode. The scanning electron microscopic (SEM) image shows that the attapulgite can be uniformly distributed on the electrode surface and form a layer of even film (Fig. 1C). This feature is very useful for the nanostructures being used as a substrate for the adsorption of TNT because the uniform nanostructure can significantly increase the effective surface of the electrode and enhance the loading of TNT, thereby improving the electrochemical response. The attapulgite on the surface of the GC electrode is fairly stable because the electrochemical response of the attapulgite/GC electrode after 200cycles of continuous scanning in PBS remained almost unchanged except for a slight decrease at the initial five cycles (decrease by approximately 5%). Moreover, the electrochemical response of the attapulgite/GC electrode stored in a desiccator for four weeks also does not almost change compared to that of the electrode prepared immediately.

The electrochemical measurements were performed with an Autolab PGSTAT302N electrochemical station (Metrohm) in a two-compartment three-electrode cell with a sample volume of ∼10mL at ambient temperature. The working electrode was an attapulgite/GC electrode. A Pt foil (1cm×1cm, Metrohm) and an Ag/AgCl electrode (saturated with KCl solution, Metrohm) were used as a counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. PBS (0.1M, pH7.0) was used as a supporting electrolyte, which was purged with high purity nitrogen for at least 30min prior to measurements, and a nitrogen atmosphere was streamed over the solution to exclude oxygen. First, TNT was accumulated onto an electrode surface from a stirred aqueous TNT solution (250rpm, 100ppb) by biasing the immersed electrode at a constant potential of 0V (vs/ Ag/AgCl) for a certain time (0–60s). Next, the electrode with adsorbed TNT was taken out (not under potential control), washed with doubly distilled water, and immersed into aqueous PBS solution with no TNT for electrochemical measurements using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), which was performed by scanning the electrode potential from −0.2 to −0.85V at a scan rate of 50mV/s. All electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature (22±1°C).

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were recorded using a JSM-7600F field-emitting scanning electron microscope. UV-Vis spectra measurements were performed on a Cary 5000 UV-Vis-NIR spectrometer (Varian). The FTIR transmission spectra were recorded at a resolution of 4cm−1 using a Nexus 670 FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet Instruments) for sampling on a KBr disk. X-ray (XRD) diffraction patterns of the attapulgite were obtained on a Rigaku/Max-3A X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.15418nm). The ζ potential measurement and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis were carried out on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Analyzer (Malvern).

Before the electrochemical detection of the TNT using the attapulgite/GC electrode, the adsorption of the TNT on the surface of attapulgite was studied. The attapulgite/GC electrode was first immersed into the TNT solution (100ppb) for 40s at a bias of 0V for TNT accumulation at the surface of attapulgite. It was then taken out and rinsed with distilled water to remove any loosely adsorbed TNT molecules. After that, the FTIR spectrum of the TNT-adsorbed attapulgite was recorded and compared with those of TNT and pure attapulgite. The FTIR spectrum of TNT-adsorbed attapulgite (curve c, Fig. 2A) shows the characteristic absorption bands of the stretching and plane bending vibration of the SiO group (~982, 1203, 480, and 1034cm−1), the stretching vibration of the –OH group (~3552 and 3381cm−1), and the deformation mode of adsorbed H2O molecules on the attapulgite surface (~1656cm−1) [37,38]. These absorption bands are the same as those for pure attapulgite (curve a, Fig. 2A). The FTIR spectrum of the TNT-adsorbed attapulgite also shows the stretching vibration mode (~1603 and 1619cm−1), breathing mode (~1170cm−1), and in-plane band mode (~714cm−1) of the phenyl ring. Moreover, it also shows the symmetric stretching, asymmetric stretching, and out-of-plane vibration modes of the NO2 group at ~1353, 1539, and 732cm−1, respectively. A series of weak absorption bands corresponding to CH (in methyl and in ring) stretching modes appear in the range from ~2922 to 3101cm−1, and peaks corresponding to the in-plane and out-of-plane vibration modes of the CH group (in ring) appear at ~1086 and 907cm−1 [47–49], respectively. These absorption bands are the same as those characteristic absorption bands of TNT molecules as depicted in curve b in Fig. 2A. Therefore, the FTIR spectrum of the attapulgite (TNT-adsorbed attapulgite) exhibits the absorption features of the addition of attapulgite characteristics into TNT characteristics, indicative of the adsorption of TNT on attapulgite surface.

The adsorption of the TNT on the surface of attapulgite was further evidenced based on the changes in the absorption intensity in the UV-vis spectra of the TNT solution before and after accumulation by the attapulgite/GC electrode. The UV-vis spectrum of pure attapulgite displays a featureless curve in the studied wavelength range (curve a, Fig. 2B). The UV-vis spectrum of TNT shows an absorption peak at ~220nm with a shoulder at ~254nm (curve c, Fig. 2B). The spectrum is flat in the visible range, which is consistent with its visible colorless quality. After being accumulated by the attapulgite/GC electrode, the UV-vis absorption peak positions of the TNT do not change (curve b, Fig. 2B), remaining the same as that before accumulation. However, the intensity of the absorption peak at ~220nm decreases by ~14.6% after accumulation, implying that the concentration of the TNT solution decreases by ~14.6ppb caused by the accumulation of the TNT on the surface of the attapulgite. This result suggests a higher adsorption tendency of the TNT on attapulgite, which should be attributed to the high adsorption ability of attapulgite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of TNT using the attapulgite/GC electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) of TNT on the attapulgite/GC electrode was performed to study the electrochemical activity of the electrode for the TNT reduction. First, the accumulation of TNT on the attapulgite/GC electrode was performed by immersing the electrode into PBS containing 100ppb TNT (pH7.0). After 40s of accumulation at a constant potential of 0V (vs. Ag/AgCl), the electrode was taken out (without being under potential control) and washed with distilled water. It was then was put into PBS without TNT, and the LSV was recorded by scanning the electrode potential from −0.2 to −0.85V at a scan rate of 50mV/s. There are three well-defined reduction peaks observed in the LSV curve located at approximately −0.36, −0.52, and −0.70V (curve a, Fig. 3 ). However, if the attapulgite/GC electrode was not loaded with TNT by immersion in TNT solution at 0V, then there are no reduction peaks in the LSV curve (curve c, Fig. 3). Instead, the LSV appears as a smooth and featureless curve over the entire potential range (−0.2 to −0.85V). These results suggest that the three reduction peaks depicted in curve a (Fig. 3) can be assigned to the stepwise reduction of the three nitro groups in the TNT molecule toward the hydroxylamines, followed by the conversion of the latter to the amine groups, which are consistent with previous electrochemical studies [22–24]. The electrochemical reduction of each nitro group to amine group can be expressed by Eqs. 1–3 [31,50].
(1)
R –
NO 2
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e −
→ R – NO +
H 2
O
(2)
R – NO + 2
H +
+ 2
e −
→ R – NHOH +
H 2
O
(3)
R – NHOH + 2
H +
+ 2
e −
→ R –
NH 2
+
H 2
O

The reduction of one nitro group to the amino group is a process requiring six electrons and six protons in total. It can be resolved in three steps, which implies the existence of two intermediates. In the first step, the reduction of one nitro group proceeds via the 2e−/2H+ electron and proton transfers to yield (upon the elimination of the water molecule) the nitroso intermediate. Since the nitroso group is more easily reducible than the nitro group, it is immediately further reduced by the 2e−/2H+ process into a hydroxylamine intermediate. Finally, the hydroxylamine intermediate is reduced by the two electrons into the aromatic amine. Again, this process requires two protons and involves then elimination of the water molecule.

To confirm that TNT can be stably adsorbed on the attapulgite/GC electrode, we designed a control experiment. The attapulgite/GC electrode was accumulated in TNT (pH7.0, 100ppb) for 40s at 0V, then the electrode was taken out under potential control (at 0V) and washed with distilled water. Finally, the electrode was put into PBS (without TNT) to record the LSV response. The obtained LSV response for the electrode transferred and washed under potential control is the same as that depicted in curve a in Fig. 3 (without being under potential control), implying that the adsorption of TNT on the attapulgite/GC electrode is stable.

To evaluate the effects of the substrates on the observed TNT reduction, we recorded the LSV responses of TNT electrochemical reduction at the bare GC and compared it with that obtained at the attapulgite/GC electrode. The bare GC electrode was first loaded with TNT by soaking it in TNT solution (100ppb, PBS) for 40s at a constant potential of 0V, and the LSV was subsequently recorded in PBS without TNT. Although there are three reduction peaks appearing in the LSV of the bare GC electrode (curve b, Fig. 3), their intensities are too weak to be quantitatively analyzed, implying low electrocatalytic activity of the bare GC electrode for the TNT reduction.

The performance of the attapulgite/GC electrode toward the electrochemical reduction of TNT can be affected by a variety of parameters, such as accumulation time, solution pH, etc. This work optimized these conditions. Increasing the accumulation time leads to an enhancement in the current response for the reduction of TNT, and the response reaches a maximum at an accumulation time of 30–40s (Fig. 4A). Therefore, an accumulation time of 40 is chosen as the optimal accumulation time for the detection of TNT. This accumulation time is shorter than those obtained at graphene sheet (60s) [23], N-GQDs (150s) [24], and IL-modified carbon nanotubes (150s) [22], suggesting a rapid adsorption of TNT at the attapulgite/GC electrode. Another optimized parameter was the solution pH. In the range of pH variant from 5 to 9, an optimal response is found at a pH of approx. 6–7 (Fig. 4B). Accordingly, a pH7 buffer solution was used for the electrochemical detection of TNT.

Fig. 4C shows the LSV response of the attapulgite/GC electrode with increasing concentration of TNT in the accumulation solution under optimal accumulation conditions. The reduction current gradually increases with increasing concentration of TNT. We choose the cathodic peak at −0.36V for our assay signal, which linearly responds to TNT at a lower concentration and attains a saturation level at a higher concentration (Fig. 4D). The peak current displays a strong linear range from 1 to 200ppb with a fit equation of j (current density, in μAcm−2)=0.048+7.413×10−4 c (TNT concentration, in ppb) and correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998. The detection limit is estimated to be 0.2ppb at a signal/noise (S/N) ratio of 3, which is low enough for the practical detection of TNT in drinking water, as 2ppb of TNT is the maximum concentration allowed in drinking water by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [13].

The detection limit of the attapulgite/GC electrode is considerably better than the previously reported TNT sensors (both electrochemical and nonelectrochemical) (Table 1 ). For example, the detection limit of our electrode is lower than that of the electrochemical TNT sensor based on VO2 (1ppb) [34], IL-graphene (4ppb) [22], and SiO2 (1.6ppb) [33]. It is also lower than that of the SERS sensor based on ZnO-Ag nanohybrids (1.1ppb) [51] and comparable to that of fluorescent sensor based on silica nanoparticles (0.2ppb) [52]. These results indicate that our developed attapulgite/GC electrode is competitive among many previously reported sensors for TNT detection.

Different aspects regarding the characteristics of the attapulgite/GC electrode were evaluated. The RSD (relative standard deviation) was estimated to be ~1.2% from slopes of calibration plots for five different and freshly prepared electrodes, revealing an acceptable reproducibility in the construction of the electrode. The assay precision of the electrode was examined by five determinations at a TNT concentration of 100ppb. A RSD of ∼0.5% is obtained. The stability of the electrode was also considered. We stored it in buffer at 4°C when the electrode was not in use and recorded its response to 100ppb TNT solution over 1–2day intervals. The response decreases slightly in the first few days (∼3days), and afterward the response tends to be practically constant; the electrode still retains ∼94% of its original response even after 1month of storage. After the electrode has been used continually for 1week (two measurements a day), its response still remains at ∼90% of the initial value. These results mean that the electrode can be used as a stable sensing platform for TNT detection.

The attapulgite/GC elctrode also shows high selectivity for TNT among some other nitroaromatic compounds and anti-interfering ability to common water contaminants. Fig. 5 depicts the LSV response of 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT) and 2-nitrotoluene (2-NT) at the attapulgite/GC elctrode. It is observed that 2,4-DNT exhibits two reduction peaks at approximately −0.54 and −0.66V (Fig. 5A), while 2-NT shows only one reduction peak at approximately −0.69V (Fig. 5B), indicating that the features of the LSV response of these nitroaromatic compounds, including the reduction peak potentials and especially the number of reduction peaks, depend on the number of nitro groups in the molecule. As a result, the different nitroaromatic compounds can easily be distinguished from their electrochemical characteristics at the attapulgite/GC electrode. Therefore, the electrode has high selectivity in the electrochemical determination of TNT.

The anti-interferring ability of the electrode to certain common water contaminants was evaluated by recording its LSV response in the PBS containing different contaminants, including benzidine (50ppb), phenol (50ppb), hydrazine (50ppb), Cu2+ ions (500ppb), Fe3+ ions (500ppb), Pb2+ ions (500ppb), Zn2+ ions (500ppb), and NO3 − ions (500ppb). It is observed that the presence of these contaminants does not affect the LSV response of TNT on the attapulgite/GC electrode. Such behavior is attributed to the low reduction potential of TNT [53]. Further studies indicate that high levels of common surfactants such as Triton X-100 (100ppb) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (100ppb) in PBS can reduce the LSV response of the attapulgite/GC electrode by ~4 and 13%, respectively. Because the surfactants in water samples are mostly at a lower level, such depressions are not of major concern. Therefore, these results suggest the high anti-interfering ability of the attapulgite/GC electrode to work for an assay platform of TNT over a wide range of potential contaminants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the TNT in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the attapulgite/GC electrode for TNT detection in a real water sample, we studied three water samples: tap water, groundwater, and lake water. These samples were first contaminated with 2, 5, and 10ppb TNT, and the recovery measurements were subsequently carried out. The average recoveries for the three different samples (2, 5, and 10ppb TNT) are 98.6, 99.6, and 98.4%, respectively, and the RSDs of the recoveries are 1.21, 2.66, and 1.07% (Table 2 ), respectively. These results indicate reliability of the assay based on the attapulgite/GC electrode.

A natural mineral attapulgite modified glassy carbon electrode (attapulgite/GC) was demonstrated to have high ability for TNT adsorption and high electrochemical activity for TNT reduction. This electrode has been used as a platform for the highly sensitive and selective detection of TNT with a low detection limit, acceptable stability and repeatability, and strong suitability for detecting TNT in real water samples. Considering the fact that attapulgite is natural clay with high abundance, low price, and environmental friendliness, attapulgite can be a practical electrochemical platform for creating a sensitive, reliable, convenient, and inexpensive assay of trace TNT in drinking water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by NSFC (21273117, 21375063, 21335004, and 21405083), NSF of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (14KJB150012), and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

sb:author.ce:surname: Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry

sb:publisher.sb:name: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Innovative Treatment Technologies: Annual Status Report
